did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and computer vision techniques for skin lesion localization, classification, and segmentation. Specifically, it employs U-Net models for automatic segmentation and optimizes hyperparameters of five pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models (VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, and MobileNet) using the Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) algorithm to classify melanoma skin cancer using the 'Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images' and 'Skin Cancer ISIC' datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel approach for skin lesion detection, classification, and segmentation using deep learning and Harris Hawks Optimization. It details specific methodologies (U-Net, CNNs, HHO), describes experiments conducted on two specific datasets ('Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images' and 'Skin Cancer ISIC'), and reports original performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, etc.) and comparisons with other studies, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An aseptic approach towards skin lesion localization and grading using deep learning and harris hawks optimization
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated approach for skin lesion localization, classification, and segmentation to address the challenges of early melanoma detection. The methodology combines U-Net models for automatic segmentation with five pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, and MobileNet) optimized via the Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) algorithm. The models were evaluated on two public datasets: the 'Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images' and the 'Skin Cancer ISIC' dataset. The DenseNet169 model achieved the best performance on the melanoma dataset with 97.08% accuracy, while MobileNet performed best on the ISIC dataset with 96.06% accuracy. Comparative analysis against nine related studies demonstrated the efficiency and robustness of the proposed hybrid framework.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as the primary malignant class in the 'Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images' (5,105 malignant images) and as one of 9 classes in the 'Skin Cancer ISIC' dataset (439 images). The paper focuses on melanoma detection and classification.

Studied as benign classes in the 'Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images' (5,500 benign images) and as specific classes in the 'Skin Cancer ISIC' dataset: Nevus (358), Pigmented Benign Keratosis (463), Seborrheic Keratosis (78), Dermatofibroma (96), Actinic Keratosis (115), Basal Cell Carcinoma (377), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (182), and Vascular Lesion (140).

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. benign, or 9-class skin cancer types)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs classification using 5 pre-trained CNN models (VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, and MobileNet) optimized by Harris Hawks Optimization. It reports accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score for binary (melanoma vs. benign) and multi-class (9 classes in ISIC dataset) tasks.

The paper utilizes U-Net models for automatic segmentation and a manual approach for datasets without masks. Performance is reported using loss, accuracy, Mean Absolute Error, Mean Squared Error, Mean Squared Logarithmic Error, and Root Mean Squared Error.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images
datasets_used.description: A public dataset acquired from Kaggle, consisting of dermoscopy images of skin lesions. It is partitioned into 2 classes: malignant and benign.

use.training: Used for training and evaluating the proposed CNN models (VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, MobileNet) optimized by HHO.
use.validation: Used for validation during the hyperparameter optimization process.
use.testing: Used for testing to report performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score).

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer ISIC
datasets_used.description: A public dataset acquired from Kaggle (referenced as 'Skin cancer isic' by Katanskiy A, 2019), consisting of dermoscopy images. It is partitioned into 9 classes of skin cancer types.

use.training: Used for training and evaluating the proposed CNN models (VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, MobileNet) optimized by HHO.
use.validation: Used for validation during the hyperparameter optimization process.
use.testing: Used for testing to report performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) and qualitative statements regarding efficiency.

segmentation.notes: Segmentation was performed using U-Net models. The best-reported scores for segmentation are listed above.

classification.notes: DenseNet169 achieved the best reported scores: 97.08% accuracy, 98.50% precision, 95.38% sensitivity, 98.65% specificity, and 96.92% F1-score.

segmentation.notes: The U-Net model was optimized on the 'Skin Cancer ISIC' dataset. The reported metrics are identical to those reported for the melanoma dataset segmentation in the text, though the text implies these are the best-reported scores for the segmentation task generally.

classification.notes: MobileNet achieved the best reported scores: 96.06% accuracy, 83.05% precision, 81.05% sensitivity, 97.93% specificity, and 82.03% F1-score.

architecture_used.main_model: Five pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet169, DenseNet201, MobileNet) optimized by Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO); U-Net for segmentation

preprocessing_techniques: Data scaling: Normalization, Standardization, Min–Max Scaling, Max-Absolute Scaling, and Robust Scaling

Manual segmentation preprocessing: Linear contrast correction (min-max scaling), conversion to grayscale, Gaussian blurring (5x5 kernel), adaptive thresholding (Gaussian-weighted sum, C=3, neighborhood size 125), erosion and dilation (3 iterations), contour extraction and sorting by area
Automatic segmentation: U-Net model with VGG19 backbone, GeLU hidden activation, Sigmoid output activation, batch normalization, and Adam optimizer

Data augmentation: Horizontal flip, Vertical flip, Rotation range [0°, 45°], Width shift range [0, 0.25], Height shift range [0, 0.25], Shear range [0, 0.25], Zoom range [0, 0.25], Brightness range [0.5, 2.0]
Input resizing: 128x128x3 for classification models; 256x256x3 for segmentation models

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The study included 'Apply Data Augmentation' as a hyperparameter optimized by the Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) algorithm, with possible values of [Yes, No]. Augmentation was applied in 4 out of the 5 CNN experiments on the Melanoma dataset.
Rotation Range optimized within [0°, 45°] with a step of 1°.
Horizontal Flip Range optimized with possible values of [Yes, No].
Vertical Flip Range optimized with possible values of [Yes, No].

conclusions: The proposed aseptic approach combining deep learning and Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) is efficient for skin lesion detection, classification, and segmentation.

For the 'Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images', the DenseNet169 model optimized by HHO achieved the best performance with 97.08% accuracy, 98.50% precision, 95.38% sensitivity, 98.65% specificity, and 96.92% F1-score.

For the 'Skin Cancer ISIC' dataset, the MobileNet model optimized by HHO achieved the best performance with 96.06% accuracy, 83.05% precision, 81.05% sensitivity, 97.93% specificity, and 82.03% F1-score.

The U-Net model achieved segmentation scores of 91.95% accuracy, 0.15908 loss, 0.08864 Mean Absolute Error, 0.04313 Mean Squared Error, 0.02072 Mean Squared Logarithmic Error, and 0.20767 Root Mean Squared Error.

Comparative analysis against nine related studies demonstrates that the proposed framework achieves accuracy levels among the best reported, proving its efficiency and applicability in medical systems.
The study acknowledges that automatic detection, classification, and segmentation of skin lesions remains an open-ended research area requiring further investigation.
important_remarks: The study relies on two public datasets acquired from Kaggle ('Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images' and 'Skin Cancer ISIC') rather than a single, standardized benchmark like HAM10000, which may limit direct comparability with other state-of-the-art studies.

The 'Skin Cancer ISIC' dataset used is a small, curated subset of 2,248 images with significant class imbalance (e.g., Seborrheic Keratosis has only 78 images vs. Basal Cell Carcinoma with 377), potentially biasing performance metrics.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons are based on descriptive metrics and qualitative statements.

The Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) algorithm was used to optimize hyperparameters with a very small population size (10) and only 10 iterations, which may be insufficient for robust global optimization in complex deep learning spaces.

The segmentation performance metrics (e.g., U-Net accuracy of 91.95%) are reported identically for both datasets, raising questions about whether the same model was applied or if there is a reporting inconsistency.
The study acknowledges that automatic detection, classification, and segmentation of skin lesions remains an open-ended research area, indicating limited generalizability of the proposed framework.
No external validation was performed; the models were only evaluated on the two internal datasets, limiting the assessment of clinical relevance and generalizability to diverse populations.

The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed cross-validation strategies, relying instead on an 85/15 train-test split, which may not fully prevent overfitting given the small dataset sizes.
